title: RAID Rebuild Time Guide
summary: Understand drive size, rebuild speed, array utilization, RAID type, verification behavior, and degraded-state exposure.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates how long a RAID array may remain degraded or rebuilding after a disk failure, including workload impact and optional verify/scrub time.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning storage arrays, evaluating large-disk RAID sets, documenting recovery exposure, or explaining why rebuild time matters in compute/storage designs.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible RAID Rebuild Time inputs and explain how each value affects recovery exposure.

items.label: Drive Size (TB)
items.description: The capacity of the drive being rebuilt or replaced.
items.whyItMatters: Larger drives usually keep the array in a degraded state longer.
items.commonMistake: Choosing large disks only by cost per TB and ignoring rebuild exposure.

items.label: Rebuild Speed (MB/s)
items.description: The sustained rebuild rate available after controller limits, media speed, workload impact, and throttling are considered.
items.whyItMatters: Rebuild speed directly controls how long the array remains vulnerable.
items.commonMistake: Assuming rebuild uses full advertised drive speed.

items.label: Array Utilization During Rebuild
items.description: How much normal production load is expected while the array is rebuilding.
items.whyItMatters: Active workloads can slow rebuilds and increase user-visible performance impact.
items.commonMistake: Estimating rebuild time on an idle system and applying it to production.

items.label: RAID Type
items.description: The redundancy model used by the array.
items.whyItMatters: RAID type affects rebuild behavior, fault tolerance, parity overhead, and risk during degraded operation.
items.commonMistake: Choosing RAID only by usable capacity.

items.label: Verify / Scrub After Rebuild?
items.description: Whether the design includes verification, scrub, or consistency checking after the rebuild operation.
items.whyItMatters: Verification can extend the recovery workflow and keep storage under maintenance pressure longer.
items.commonMistake: Ignoring post-rebuild verification time when documenting recovery exposure.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means rebuild time is likely manageable. Watch means degraded-state exposure should be reviewed. Risk means the array may remain vulnerable long enough to materially increase operational risk.
bullets: Rebuild hours are only useful if the rebuild speed assumption is realistic.
RAID type changes both capacity and failure exposure.
Long rebuild windows should trigger a review of backups, spares, and fault domains.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: RAID Rebuild Time checks the recovery side of the compute storage design after capacity and performance have already been estimated.
bullets: Use storage throughput context when estimating rebuild speed.
Carry degraded-state exposure into Backup Window planning.
A storage design is not complete until failure and recovery behavior are reviewed.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
